EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 45 year-old man was struck by lightning while leaning against a chain-link fence at his residence at 89-155 Nanaikala Place in Nanakuli.  The man was hospitalized and observed for several hours and then released.
A funnel cloud was sighted by police in the vicinity of Kilauea Lighthouse.

Easterly swell was generated by Tropical Cyclone Guillermo when it was most intense some 2000 miles east of Hawaii.  The swell produced surf of 6 to 8 feet with occasional sets to 10 feet along the east facing shores of the island from Upolu Point to Cape Kumukahi to South Point.  Observers in Orchidland reported surf heights to 10 feet.
Two stationary waterspouts were observed over the Alenuihaha Channel.

An upper level low from northwest of the Hawaiian Islands cut-off just north of the state and persisted in the area from the 15th through the 17th.   During this period, moisture from the remnants of Tropical Cyclone Guillermo combined with an unstable airmass due to the close proximity of the upper level low to help fuel widely scattered heavy showers and thunderstorms over all of the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers produced minor street flooding in and near Lanai City  (Lanai) and Maunaloa (Molokai).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed from the Lanai City Airport.  The funnel cloud was initially stationary, then moved slowly toward the southeast before dissipating.

Significant road and stream flooding occurred from heavy showers and thunderstorms in the Makakilo and Nanakuli areas.  Water to 4 feet deep flooded parts of the Farrington Highway in Nanakuli.  A  WSR-88D rainfall estimate of 6 inches per hour was corroborated by a weather spotter in Makakilo who measured 5.8 inches during the same time period.
Minor road and stream flooding reported throughout the area from Pukalani to Makawao to Haiku.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred across the Hamakua, Hilo, Puna, and Kau Districts with widespread minor street flooding and stream flooding north of Hilo where some streams were blocked by debris.

High water washed away a culvert bridge leaving a 20 foot gap in Waha Place road leading to a cul-de-sac off of Ulumalu Road between the Hana Highway and Five Corners.  10 families living in homes around the cul-de-sac  were stranded for about 9 hours.  At 2:30 a.m., a house was severely flooded by a debris blocked stream in the Honopua area on the mountain side of Hana Highway just before Twin Falls.  Contents of the house and a pickup truck were destroyed.  The Hana Highway was blocked in several spots by landslides, and the Piilani Highway was closed due to high water and damage by erosion between Kipahulu and Kaupo.

Several houses in the Hilo and Puna Districts had flooded basements or ground floors. About a dozen cars were flooded out near Pauahi Street and Kamehameha Avenue in Hilo.  In Puna, North Kulani Road, Hawaiian Acres and Fern Acres subdivisions were flooded.  The Hawaiian Belt Road through the Kau District was flooded out in several locations between Naalehu and Pahala.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Piilani Highway south from Hana to Kipahulu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and scattered thunderstorms associated with a tropical disturbance tracking to the south of  the islands of Hawaii and Maui brought copious amounts of rain to parts of both islands late on the 29th and the 30th.  In the 24 hour period ending at 2 p.m. on the 30th, the following rainfall amounts were recorded: Glenwood, 12.27 inches, Mountain View, 11.98 inches, Waiakea Uka, 10.79 inches, Pahala, 8.65 inches, Piihonua, 8.01 inches, Laupahoehoe, 7.43 inches, and Hilo, 7.06 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor road flooding was reported in the South Kona District and the south part of the Kau District near Naalehu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drainageways overflowed in Lahaina, flooding an underground parking lot and damaging several cars.  Flooding closed a section of Kamehameha V Highway two miles east of Kaunakakai.Overall, residents and businesses reported losing hundreds of thousands of dollars from waters that inundated Lahaina town's low-lying Malu-ulu-o-Lele Park area between Prison and Shaw streets and on Wainee Street.

Lifeguards assisted over 30 people in distress near Oahu's shores on the 13th.

Additional rains in the Hilo and Puna Districts, with nearly 4 inches recorded at the Hilo Airport between 2 a.m. and 8 a.m., caused minor flooding of streets and low lying areas in both districts.  A landslide at Maulua Gulch just southeast of Laupahoehoe closed one lane of Mamalahoa Highway for a short time, and a second landslide closed a four mile stretch of highway between Pepeekeo and Papaikou.  The subdivisions of Hawaiian Acres and Orchidland Estates in Puna were closed due to flooded roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several low pressure centers moving across the north central Pacific generated swell that produced surf of 8 to 12 feet with occasional sets to 15 feet along the north and west facing shores of the islands.    Four  tourists, 3 from Georgia and 1 from Utah, drowned in the high surf at Keanae Point, Maui at  approximately  2 p.m. HST January 30, 1997.  One person was swept into the ocean while taking pictures of the high surf.  He was pulled from the water and given CPR by  a visiting paramedic.   While performing CPR, a second wave swept both individuals and six other people into the ocean.     Four of the eight  people were rescued by local residents, while the other four drowned.

A storm low far north of the Hawaiian Islands generated swell that produced surf of 10 to 15 feet along north  facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores of the islands.
A storm low far north of the Hawaiian Islands generated swell that produced surf of 10 to 15 feet along north  facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores of the islands.
The Kahana Stream flooded an area about 75 yards just to the mountain side of the Kamehameha Hwy.  Street flooding was reported in Kaaawa and Waikane.
Minor street flooding in many areas throughout Oahu.

Roads were closed by minor flooding in the Kihei, Makena, and Kahului areas, and through parts of central Maui.  Heavy rains on the Kaupo Coast caused Nuanualoa Stream to flood, prompting the closure of Piilani Highway between Kaupo and Kipahulu.
Lightning struck a transformer at the Rockwell Power Systems station near the summit of Haleakala, shutting down electrical power at the station for over 16 hours.
Minor street flooding was reported in Maili, Kaakako, and Kailua.

Heavy showers caused minor street flooding in Kihei and from Lahaina to Napili. The Molokai WSR-88D indicated 3.5 to 4 inches of rainfall in six hours over isolated areas of east Maui along the south slopes of Haleakala.
Heavy showers in the Lihue area caused minor flooding of roads.  Additional showers over higher terrain to the northwest brought heavy runoff and minor flooding along the Hanamaulu Stream.
Heavy showers produced runoff and minor flooding in the North and South Kona Districts.
Roads near Naalehu were flooded by heavy showers for several hours.
Minor road flooding in the South and North Kona Districts between Keahole Airport and Captain Cook.
Minor flooding of streets and low lying areas across western and central Oahu, especially in Waianae and Waipahu.
A storm low far north of the Hawaiian Islands generated swell that produced surf of 10 to 15 feet along north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores of the islands.
Nearly stationary heavy showers and thunderstorms over the northeast part of the island caused minor flooding of streams from Anahola to Kapaa.
Heavy showers flooded streets in the Wahiawa and Laie areas.  The Molokai WSR-88D estimated rainfall rates of 2 inches per hour from Halemano across the Koolau Range to near Laie.
Nearly 3 inches of rain in 4 hours caused widespread minor flooding of streets and low lying areas in Kailua-Kona Town.

Several low pressure centers moving across the north central Pacific generated swell that produced surf of 8 to 12 feet with occasional sets to 15 feet along the north and west facing shores of the islands.    Four  tourists, 3 from Georgia and 1 from Utah, drowned in the high surf at Keanae Point, Maui at  approximately  2 p.m. HST January 30, 1997.  One person was swept into the ocean while taking pictures of the high surf.  He was pulled from the water and given CPR by  a visiting paramedic.   While performing CPR, a second wave swept both individuals and six other people into the ocean.     Four of the eight  people were rescued by local residents, while the other four drowned.

An intense storm that was originally Typhoon David moved across the north Pacific near the Aleutian Islands and generated swell that produced surf of 15 to 20 feet along the north shores of the islands.  This was the first large surf of the season for the north shores.  Over 30 surfers, bodyboarders, and swimmers were rescued from Sunset Beach and Waimea Bay on Oahu.  Public beach parks were closed along Oahu's North Shore, and at Yokohama Bay on the Leeward Coast.  Surf of 8 to 12 feet occurred along the west shores, but decreased to heights below 10 feet by 6 p.m. on the 24th as the northwest swell became more northerly.
A nearly stationary waterspout was spotted by a pilot just offshore of Kaloli Point on the Puna Coast.
An extratropical low - former Typhoon Ginger - far to the northwest of the Hawaiian Islands generated swell that produced surf of 8 to 12 feet along the north shores of Kauai and Oahu.

Deep storm low that moved from near the dateline far northwest of the Hawaiian Islands to the Gulf of Alaska produced surf of 10 to 15 feet on north facing shores and 12 to 18 feet on west shores of all the islands.  The rough surf claimed the life of a 46-year old woman and injured her 40-year old female cousin in waters near Kokee Beach on the north shore of Maui.  This occurred at about 3:30 P.M. on the 22nd.  The women were caught in a strong undertow and swept out into the pounding surf.  The woman who survived was totally exhausted and barely able to stay afloat when rescuers arrived on the scene.  They were unable to resuscitate the other victim.   A male in his early 20s, one of 18 surfers to be rescued by water safety officials on the 22nd in Oahu, was injured in Makaha on the west shore of that isle in the early afternoon when he was driven into his surf board and suffered a cut underneath his eye and minor neck injuries.

Southwest winds of 60 to 80 mph buffeted the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.The combination of a surface low to the west of Kauai and an associated upper level trough brought inclement weather to the Hawaiian Islands from the 18th through much of the 20th.  This included scattered thunderstorms over the islands from the 18th through the early morning hours of the 19th.  Lightning strikes caused one slight injury and limited property damage on the Island of Hawaii and in Maui.  Also, a thunderstorm dropped 3/4-inch hail, a rare event in the state, on two communities in the southwestern part of the Island of Hawaii.  The surface low moved north and then east before dissipating north of the island chain on the 19th.  The upper level trough finally moved east of the islands late on the 20th, allowing the winds near the volcano summits on the Island of Hawaii to abate.
Nickel-size hail was reported near this South Kona community.

Storm low that deepened to the northeast of the Hawaiian Islands caused surf of 4 to 8 feet, with occasional higher sets, along the north and northeast facing shores of the Island of Hawaii and Maui.  The high surf closed Hilo's Bayfront Highway.

Deep storm low that moved from near the dateline far northwest of the Hawaiian Islands to the Gulf of Alaska produced surf of 10 to 15 feet on north facing shores and 12 to 18 feet on west shores of all the islands.  The rough surf claimed the life of a 46-year old woman and injured her 40-year old female cousin in waters near Kokee Beach on the north shore of Maui.  This occurred at about 3:30 P.M. on the 22nd.  The women were caught in a strong undertow and swept out into the pounding surf.  The woman who survived was totally exhausted and barely able to stay afloat when rescuers arrived on the scene.  They were unable to resuscitate the other victim.   A male in his early 20s, one of 18 surfers to be rescued by water safety officials on the 22nd in Oahu, was injured in Makaha on the west shore of that isle in the early afternoon when he was driven into his surf board and suffered a cut underneath his eye and minor neck injuries.

Scattered heavy showers caused widespread minor flooding of streets and low-lying areas in the lower elevations of the North and South Kohala, North and South Kona, Kau, Puna, and North and South Hilo districts.
